findings.description: fetch_events docstring promises an inclusive '[start, end]' range with 'both boundary days included', but the SQL uses 'created_at < end', so every event on the end day is silently excluded — and because end.isoformat() yields a bare date ('2026-06-10') while created_at is a datetime string ('2026-06-10 14:30:00'), the string comparison drops the entire end day, producing an off-by-one-day undercount that contradicts the documented contract.

top_users uses 'created_at < end' with a bare-date end bound, excluding the end day's events and contradicting the module's documented inclusive '[start, end]' contract from fetch_events, so the leaderboard undercounts activity on the boundary day inconsistently with the rest of the report.

event_count uses 'created_at < end' (both branches, lines 82 and 88) with a bare-date end bound, excluding the end day and undercounting versus the inclusive range the module documents — and these counts will disagree with the row set returned by fetch_events for the same window.

render_activity_report interpolates user-controlled display_name and action (lines 50-52) and title (line 54) into HTML with no html.escape(), so any value containing '<', '>', '&', or quotes corrupts the report output and enables stored XSS in the admin dashboard — a correctness defect in a report generator as well as a security hole (defer detail to critic-security).

top_users does not validate 'limit'; in SQLite a negative LIMIT means 'no limit' (returns every grouped row, not the top N) and limit=0 returns nothing, so an unexpected caller value produces a silently wrong-sized result instead of an error.

save_report performs no error handling around open()/write(), so disk-full, permission, or invalid-path failures propagate as raw OSErrors with no logging — acceptable for a library but inconsistent with the success-path logging elsewhere; borderline nit.

top_users selects u.display_name while grouping only by u.id; this is well-defined in SQLite (bare column functionally dependent on the grouped PK) so it is not a bug here, but it is non-portable to stricter engines (e.g. Postgres ONLY_FULL_GROUP_BY) — note only.
